confirmations.reason: Workspace Rules are stored in .agents/rules at the workspace or git root, which is exactly a repo-scoped file giving the agent standing instructions and context — directly matching the capability.

Plugins are described as namespaced bundles grouping skills, rules, MCP servers, and hooks into a single package — this directly matches bundling reusable capabilities/instructions the agent can load.
Lifecycle Hooks explicitly run custom local shell scripts or commands at specific lifecycle points during execution, directly matching the capability of running custom logic on agent events.

Rules provide persistent constraints and context but are not specifically about shaping the system prompt, persona, or output style. They are general instruction/constraint mechanisms, making this a category-adjacent but not direct match for output/system-prompt customization.
MCP Integration directly implements the Model Context Protocol as a standard way to connect external tools, data, and services — an exact match.
The MCP Store is explicitly a built-in store to browse, install, and authenticate MCP servers, directly realizing the capability to install, configure, scope, and discover MCP servers.
Per-Tool MCP Permission grants agent access to a single named tool on a specific MCP server, which is a direct implementation of allow-listing/governing which MCP tools the agent may use.
The Subagents feature explicitly delegates long-running operations to parallel autonomous agents, directly matching the capability of spawning focused sub-agents to handle parts of a task.

Multi-Agent Orchestration provides a framework for coordinating multiple agents with error recovery, retries, and coordination logic — directly realizing multi-agent teams sharing work and communicating.

Workflows are described as saved markdown files defining a repeatable series of steps to guide the Agent through tasks across sessions, which is a direct match for defining repeatable, named multi-step task sequences.

Request Review Mode pauses at milestones for user approval — this is approval-gating/checkpointing, not the specific plan-then-execute pattern where a full plan is produced upfront for review before any edits begin. Adjacent but not a direct match.
Worktree Support has agents operate in dedicated Git worktrees to isolate parallel work within a project, directly realizing the capability of parallel isolated workspaces without collisions.
Background Tasks explicitly run tracked, long-running operations asynchronously without blocking the primary agent — a direct match.
Scheduled Tasks allow users to schedule messages to agents with repeatable time-based triggers, directly realizing the capability of running the agent on a schedule without supervision.
Terminal Sandboxing provides kernel-level/native OS isolation for terminal commands executed by the agent, directly realizing sandboxed execution to limit blast radius.
The Fine-Grained Permissions Engine represents every sensitive agent operation as a permission, providing a unified mechanism to approve/deny what the agent is allowed to do — a direct match.

Independent Network Access Control explicitly controls network connectivity for sandboxed commands independently of filesystem restrictions, directly realizing the capability to restrict the agent's outbound network access.
Inspect Hooks are described as read-only, non-blocking hooks for logging, audit trails, and metrics collection — a direct match for audit logging and telemetry.

Observability tracks per-turn and cumulative token usage and thinking traces, which relates to usage visibility, but it does not address cost tracking or the ability to cap/limit token spend. Token usage observability is adjacent to but not the same as cost and usage tracking with caps.
